text:
2
  One of the main bottlenecks of blockchains is smart contract
y
execution. To increase throughput, modern blockchains try
a
to execute transactions in parallel. Unfortunately, however,
M common blockchain use cases introduce read-write conflicts
 
9 between transactions, forcing sequentiality.
 
   We propose RapidLane, an extension for parallel execu-
]
tion engines that allows the engine to capture computations
C
in conflicting parts of transactions and defer their execution
D until a later time, sometimes optimistically predicting execu-
.
s  tion results. This technique, coupled with support for a new
c
[ construct for smart contract languages, allows one to turn
 
  certain sequential workloads into parallelizable ones.
1  We integrated RapidLane into Block-STM, a state-of-the-
v
art parallel execution engine used by several blockchains in
7
production, and deployed it on the Aptos blockchain. Our
1
1 evaluation shows that on commonly contended workloads,
6 such as peer-to-peer transfers with a single fee payer and
0 NFT minting, RapidLane yields up to 12× more throughput.
.
5

4
2 A blockchain is a distributed system that consists of two sets
:
of nodes: validators and clients. Clients submit transactions
v
i  to validators. Validators, in turn, agree on a sequence of
X submitted transactions, and execute them in a deterministic
r
way, thereby changing the state of the blockchain.
a
While traditionally being designed as distributed ledgers
supporting only simple transfers of digital currencies, mod-
ern blockchains have a significantly broader scope of ap-
plications thanks to smart contracts — user-defined pro-
grams stored on the blockchain as bytecode [28, 66]. Popular
smart contracts include automated market makers [36], fi-
nancial derivatives [42, 65], minting non-fungible tokens
(NFTs) [4, 41], automatic insurance settlement [27], and ora-
cles to provide external off-chain data [7].
To unlock many other use cases, blockchains need to over-
come several challenges, namely low throughput. Prominent
blockchains such as Bitcoin [40] and Ethereum [62] can only
process 7 and 30 transactions per second (TPS), respectively,
compared to more than 65k TPS processed by Visa [58].

hance Smart Contract
istic Parallel Execution
abiljo  Zekun Li
Labs  Aptos Labs
a Vusirikala  Zhuolun Xiang
Labs  Aptos Labs
. Lopes  Rati Gelashvili
niversity of Lisbon  Aptos Labs
A crucial component to increase throughput, which many
modern blockchains have introduced, is a parallel execution
engine. This allows a single validator node to process transac-
tions batched in a block in parallel [16]. Unfortunately, some
of the core blockchain workloads are inherently highly con-
tended [22], creating read-write conflicts between different
transactions in a block (i.e., conflicts where one transaction
writes to and another transaction reads form the same loca-
tion in the state).
For example, many blockchains have the concept of spon-
sored transactions [3, 54, 61]. Instead of having the users
pay the transaction fees, the fees are payed by sponsors (also
called fee payers). This mechanism is used by applications
that want to cover the costs for their users, e.g., for registra-
tion. For a transaction to execute successfully, the sponsor
must have sufficient funds to cover the fees, which will be
1
charged (subtracted from the sponsor’s account balance).
Because many such transactions can be issued within a short
time to interact with the same application, they all exhibit
read-write conflicts, creating contention. Other examples
include direct transfers to or from a single account (crediting
and debiting the same balance), total token supply tracking
(every transaction updates the supply when burning a frac-
tion of the fee [14]), and NFT minting (keeping a counter to
limit the maximum number of NFTs that can be minted).
In order to avoid the problem of read-write conflicts, some
blockchains alter their systems, either removing contention
altogether (e.g., forgoing the ability to track the total supply
at any given moment in their smart contracts [49]), or sched-
uling the conflicting transactions across multiple blocks [63].
While such scheduling can lead to higher parallelism within
a single block, it has a substantial impact on user experi-
ence, as some users may need to wait a significant amount
of time until their transaction is scheduled for execution.
Moreover, it is in general not possible to accurately predict
what data a transaction will access (except for blockchains
1
Transaction fees are used to protect blockchains from denial-of-service
(DoS) attacks and to cover the infrastructure costs. They are based on
the amount of resources used and are computed during the transaction
execution to protect from infinite loops.

Mitenk
that require contract developers to manually specify an over-
approximation of the data that will be read and written).
At the same time, users currently do not have to pay higher
fees if they execute a contract that causes high contention.
As a result, smart contract developers have no incentive to
avoid data conflicts (except potentially for blockchains that
charge a fee based on the specified read/write sets).
Together with the lack of guidelines and the absence of
good developer documentation, the result is poorly-written,
sequential smart contracts, which cannot be efficiently exe-
cuted by validators even when using sophisticated parallel
execution engines. Furthermore, existing smart contract pro-
gramming languages do not have the notion of parallelism,
e.g., they do not support atomic types. Even if developers
do want to avoid contention, they have to come up with
workarounds to ensure that it is possible to execute multiple
transactions at the same time.
There exist multiple well-known techniques for optimiz-
ing programs for contended workloads, including but not
limited to sharded counters [24] or exploiting commutativity
of operations [30]. However, these techniques do not suit
well the typical blockchain workloads, as they often require
giving up key properties of computations. Reading contented
data is often unavoidable.
For example, the balance of a fee payer’s account must be
read to know it has sufficient funds, as otherwise it can make
the network vulnerable to DoS attacks. Another example is
NFT minting. A collection of NFTs is usually minted with a
specified limit on the number of tokens that can ever exist
and the index of the minted token can be used as part of its
name, e.g., “arXiv #2024” (a collection of ‘arXiv’ tokens, with
the particular token having index 2024).
Contributions The main observation of this paper is that
a large fraction of the read-write conflicts from smart con-
tracts do not have to be qualified as such. Usually, conflicting
transactions either update a value but do not need the re-
sult, or read the value before the update to check if some
precondition is satisfied (e.g., if the balance is large enough).
As a result, parallel execution engines can defer computa-
tions on contended data for every transaction until a later
time, without being required to create a read-write conflict
by reading the intermediate values. In case a precondition
must be evaluated, we find that it is usually sufficient to
predict the result and validate it at a later time.
The main contributions of this paper are:
• Deferred objects — a new concept for smart contract
programming languages that enables developers to
defer computations. With deferred objects, develop-
ers can explicitly specify objects which are expected
to be frequently mutated by transactions in parallel.
Importantly, multiple transactions updating the same
deferred object do not conflict, and the updates do not
have to be commutative. For example, Figure 1 shows

ov, Kabiljo, Li, Spiegelman, Vusirikala, Xiang, Zlateski, Lopes, and Gelashvili
Execution engine  Smart contract
-5
Estimate a = 3  0: if (a.sub(5)) {
false  1:  // ...
Same execution result  2: } else {
+10
if:  3:   a.add(10);
true  4:   // ...
a - 5 < 0 &&
5:   // ...
a + 10 <= UINT_MAX &&  -11
6:   a.sub(11);
a + 10 - 11 >= 0
true  7: }
Deferred update for a: -1
Figure 1. On the right, a snippet of smart contract code which is
being executed. The code uses a deferred object a which supports
checked arithmetic via the add and sub methods. Both methods
return true if there is no overflow or underflow. On the left, a
parallel execution engine which captures the updates to a from
the contract (−5, +10, and −11). Instead of reading the value of
a, its value is estimated to make a prediction about the outcomes
of arithmetic operations. Also, modifications to the value stored
inside the deferred object are delayed, thus eliminating read-write
conflicts if the object is shared across multiple transactions.
a snippet of a smart contract that uses deferred objects
to avoid read-write conflicts on a shared counter, and
how it can be used by the execution engine.
• RapidLane — a production-grade parallel execution
engine extension used to implement deferred objects.
RapidLane is built on top of Block-STM [20], a state-
of-the-art shared-memory parallel execution engine
adopted by multiple blockchains [38, 44, 47, 52, 55].
Thanks to deferred semantics, conflicting parts of
transactions are captured and deferred in dedicated
lanes, where they are also performed efficiently in
parallel. To increase parallelism, the execution results
are optimistically predicted as depicted in Figure 1.
• A new open-source benchmark suite containing com-
mon blockchain transactions, including real contracts
for peer-to-peer transfers, sponsored transactions, and
NFT minting.

The benchmark suite also includes syn-
thetic benchmarkswith conflictingworkloads designed
to stress test parallel execution engines. This bench-
mark suite allows drawing more qualitative compar-
isons across blockchains over single TPS numbers.
• A thorough evaluation of RapidLane on the Aptos
blockchain. We study how our systemperforms on the
benchmark suite, and additionally, conduct a series of
smaller experiments to describe the system’s behavior.
As a secondary contribution, the paper enhances Block-
STM with a rolling commit mechanism. At virtually no per-
formance cost, it allows the system to know when each
transaction is committed, as opposed to block granularity in
Block-STM’s original lazy commit mechanism..

metadata.section_title: Deferred objects: a parallel programming model for smart contracts

text:
We propose an extension to programming languages and
VM runtimes for smart contracts: deferred objects, which
are containers that capture and defer computation. In this
section, we present the concept of deferred objects with an

transactions and their execution output, from top to bottom: 1) Trans
global state. Gas is still charged, updating the user’s balance in the wri
increments the counter. 4) Transaction runs out of gas due to insuffic
example. We consider the Move smart contract program-
ming language, which encodes logic to sell a limited number
of concert tickets (see Figure 3). We use it to show how a
sequential implementation with non-trivial read-write con-
flicts and non-commutative operations can be turned into a
parallel one, thanks to a deferred counter. As we go through
the example, we also discuss the necessary interaction be-
tween the VM and the parallel execution engine. In later
sections, we define deferred objects formally and describe
the required changes in the VM implementation.
3.1  Deferred objects by example
We first consider a sequential implementation of the contract
(lines 13–27). The entity that issues the tickets is represented
by the TicketMachine struct, which stores a counter to track
the number of tickets issued so far. A concert ticket is repre-
sented as a Ticket struct.
Now, we assume there is a TicketMachine stored in the
global state, and that there are multiple transactions that call
the claim function in order to obtain a ticket for different
users. Clearly, transactions calling the claim function cannot
be executed in parallel: each of them reads and writes the
TicketMachine’s global counter.
Next, we show how to use deferred objects to remove the
read-write conflict when mutating the TicketMachine in
order to allow transactions calling the claim function to be
executed in parallel. First, both Ticket and TicketMachine
have their fields wrapped as deferred objects. The deferred
type specifies that the computations performed on these
fields are captured and deferred until commit time. A trans-
action that accesses a TicketMachine (line 35), gets the

17:  }

counter wrapped into a deferred object, without leaking
the actual value it stores.
Calling update on the deferred object tries to apply the
increment function on the wrapped counter (line 38). This
function returns a boolean indicating whether the counter
has been incremented. In practice, the execution engine run-
ning this transaction detects that an update to a deferred
object is being made, captures the computation 𝜆𝑥 . 𝑥 + 1,
and decides if the increment call succeeds or not (i.e., the
counter’s limit is exceeded). The decision is made using a
heuristic that does not require reading the most recent value
of the counter. Assuming the increment succeeds, update
returns true and the engine logs the deferred computation.
Next, the name of the ticket needs to be computed without
reading the counter value (lines 40-44). The map operation
creates a new deferred string that produces the name of the
ticket.

metadata.section_title: Deferred objects: a parallel programming model for smart contracts

text:
The call to format is deferred until commit time, and
2
a dummy string is returned to initialize the ticket (line 42).
Finally, the Ticket is published to the global state, and the
transaction execution terminates. The VM, in addition to
the write-set with a single Ticket, produces two deferred
changes: 𝜆𝑥 . 𝑥 +1 for the cnt field, and string formatting for
the name field. By deferring the read of the counter, and the
subsequent computations, the write to the TicketMachine
has been eliminated. As a result, transactions have no read-
write conflicts and can execute in parallel.
2
Note that deferring computations slows down the commit phase. In this
example, the overhead of delayed string creation can be non-negligible. A
more efficient alternative would be to have the ticket store just its number,
and have the name computed on the fly in the “getter” function. We use a
deferred string here to show the generality of deferred objects.

In this section, we present RapidLane, an extension to the
Block-STM parallel execution engine to optimize contended
workloads. RapidLane leverages deferred objects by captur-
ing the logs provided by the VM for each transaction, and
replaying them at commit time. Thanks to the multi-version
data structures of Block-STM, transactions that create logs
for the same deferred objects do not conflict (similarly to
how write-write conflicts are avoided).
In our implementation, we restrict the types that can be
wrapped as deferred objects to integer counters and small-
sized strings (up to 256 bytes). The counter’s value can be
updated with addition or subtraction, as long as it is within
some fixed lower and upper bounds. We also allow for integer
deferred objects to be mapped once.
4.1  Block-STM preliminaries
Block-STM takes a block of 𝑛 transactions [tx1, tx2, . . . , tx𝑛]
as input, and returns 𝑛 transaction outputs. The outputs are
the same as if transactions were executed in the given order
tx < tx < . . . < tx𝑛, i.e., sequentially.
1  2
The write-set produced when a transaction is executed
is recorded in a multi-versioned data-structure, MVMemory,
where writes from different transactions are stored indepen-
dently. MVMemory also exposes an interface that lets tx𝑖
read the currently “latest” value for a given key, i.e., stored
write from tx 𝑗 with highest 𝑗 < 𝑖 (or, in case there are no
previous writes, from storage, i.e., based on the pre-block
global state).
In Block-STM, transactions are executed concurrently and
thus the write-sets can be applied toMVMemory out of order.
This makes reads speculative, i.e., the results depend on the
contents of MVMemory at the time of reading and may not
yield the same result as if transactions were executed sequen-
tially. A component called CapturedReads stores information

about all reads done by transactions. This information is
later used to validate the correctness of the execution.
The scheduler provides tasks to each worker thread. An
execution task executes a transaction, with an incarnation
number equal to the number of times it has been executed
previously. It is guaranteed that incarnations of the same
transaction occur in order and are never concurrent.
A validation task validates the read-set of the transaction’s
latest incarnation, checking for conflicting writes done by
transactions that are ordered before in the block, but which
may have completed executing after the current one. Since
earlier transaction outputs may affect later transactions, the
scheduler is designed to prioritize tasks with lower transac-
tion indices. Scheduling satisfies the following:
Invariant 1. Each incarnation is validated at least once. After
completion, an incarnation of a transaction tx𝑖 gets scheduled
for (re-)validation whenever a task for transaction tx 𝑗 for 𝑗 < 𝑖
may influence the result of a read in the read-set of tx𝑖 .
When validation fails, the incarnation is aborted and the
transaction is scheduled for re-execution (with an incre-
mented incarnation number). Transactions with a higher
number are also re-validated. As a result, and as proved
in [20], Block-STM satisfies the following property:
Property 1. For any 1 ≤ 𝑖 ≤ 𝑛, the last incarnation of tx𝑖 is
validated in a global state that is consistent with the sequential
execution of transactions 1, 2, . . . , 𝑖 and never changes.
In order to describe our changes to Block-STM in a way
that is oblivious to the complex internal details, we define
the following abstract API for the scheduler:
• schedule_execution(i), schedule_validation(i)
to schedule tx𝑖 for execution or validation.
• schedule_suffix_validation(i) to schedule trans-
actions 𝑖, . . . , 𝑛 for validation.
These methods guarantee that after they are called, ei-
ther transaction 𝑖 gets validated, transaction 𝑖 gets executed,

or transactions 𝑖, . . . , 𝑛 all get validated, respectively.

While
schedule_execution and schedule_validation are more
common functionalities, schedule_suffix_validation is
specific to Block-STM (since the outputs of earlier transac-
tions affecting the later ones).
Another important aspect of Block-STM is dependency
management. When an incarnation is aborted, its writes
stored inMVMemory are marked as estimated. When a trans-
action tx 𝑗 tries to read an estimated value from a transaction
tx𝑖 with 𝑖 < 𝑗 , the execution is suspended. Execution of tx 𝑗
can resume after the next incarnation of tx𝑖 finishes and
replaces the estimated writes with the new ones. Validation
fails if it encounters an estimated write.
4.2  Compressing deferred counter logs
With deferred objects, executing transactions produces a set
of logs, in addition to a write-set. Because logs can become
large, replaying them at commit time can be slow. To avoid
that, we compress deferred counter logs by taking advantage
of the properties of integer addition and the precondition
format we support. Figure 6 shows how logs can be com-
pressed.
It is possible that the first log entry is ‘value(v)’. In this
case, the log contains all the information needed to compress
it into a single value by replay. Similarly, if the log contains
a ‘revealed’ entry, its value can be used directly.
Otherwise, the log can be compressed into a single summed
up update and 4 comparisons. Consider the 𝑖-th log entry
is an update adding 𝑥𝑖 . The precondition for the update is
𝜆𝑣 . 𝐿 ≤ 𝑣 + 𝑥𝑖 ≤ 𝑈 . We let 𝑇 be all indices of log entries,
for which the precondition was true, and 𝐹 be the indices of
entries for which it was false. We observe that summing up

where the deferred counter’s value lies. Hence, instead of
re-evaluating all preconditions that previously held true, it
is sufficient to check that 𝑥 + 𝑥𝑚𝑎𝑥 ≤ 𝑈 and 𝐿 ≤ 𝑥 − 𝑥𝑚𝑖𝑛.
If we have preconditions that were not satisfied in the
log, we need two more comparisons. A precondition might
not be satisfied because adding some 𝑥𝑖  for 𝑖 ∈ 𝐹 to the

4.3  Deltas and multi-versioning
Instead of using logs directly, RapidLane uses a more efficient
and compact data structure called a delta. Delta is a tuple of
• a unique identifier, associated with a deferred object;
• a compressed log or a revealed value.
A value can be revealed during the log compression if the log
contains a revealed entry or if the deferred object was newly
created (with an initial value). A delta in a revealed state
provides the exact underlying value of the deferred object.
On the other hand, the compressed log contains an update
(a value to add), and history constraints (4 inequalities), and
a source ID of a deferred object on top of which to apply
the updates. Unless a deferred object is created by a map
operation, the source ID is equal to the ID of the object itself.
A delta with a compressed log can be applied to any given
base value. History validation is performed first, i.e., check-
ing that the base value 𝑣 satisfies the history constraints ℎ of
the compressed log. If this history check fails during delta
application, or if during log compression the constraints im-
posed by update and reveal operations are not satisfied, the
transaction is terminated and the incarnation is marked as
speculatively failed. This can only happen due to specula-
tion in the concurrent setting, and means the transaction’s
internal state is inconsistent with any possible sequential

In our implementation, each transaction produces a delta-
set as a part of the output, consisting of deltas. Similar
to write-sets, delta-sets are recorded in a dedicated multi-
versioned data-structure, MVDelayedFields, which is ver-
sioned by transaction indices and keyed by the deferred
object ids.
MVDelayedFields exposes an interface for reading the “lat-
est” value of deferred objects. This requires finding the base
value, and potentially applying multiple deltas onto it. The
implementation works by traversing a chain of deltas.
The delta traversal by tx𝑖 of deferred object 𝐴 starts by
finding the highest index 𝑗 < 𝑖 with a recorded delta entry 𝛿
in MVDelayedFields for 𝐴 (analogous for the standard multi-
versioned data structure of Block-STM). If such a 𝑗 does not
exist, traversal ends with the base value of 𝐴 from storage.
Similarly, traversal ends if the delta entry of tx 𝑗 is contains a
revealed value. Otherwise, the delta has source ID 𝐵 and the
traversal continues by applying the same procedure for tx 𝑗
and 𝐵. When the base value is found, the deltas are applied
3
to it in the reverse order they were traversed.
We add a notable optimization to the Block-STM depen-
dency management for the MVDelayedFields data-structure.
3  𝐵  𝐴
As an optimization, in cases when  is equal to  , the traversal can avoid
performing multiple lookups by instead following an ordered iterator. Ad-
ditionally, deltas can be merged during the traversal (in a way similar to
the log compression), and then applied to the base value.

Deferred Objects to Enhance Smart Contract Programming with Optimistic P
The entries in MVDelayedFields from a particular incarna-
tion are marked as estimates when the incarnation aborts,
similar to entries in the multi-versioned data-structure in
vanilla Block-STM. Recall that in Block-STM, validation fails
upon encountering an estimate. This would be problematic
for RapidLane as delta traversals visit significantly more en-
tries in MVDelayedFields. Hence, we ignore the estimate
markings for deferred objects. Only if a re-execution of a
transaction changes the delta entry for a deferred object
(or produces a new delta entry), the optimization gets dis-
abled and the estimates start being handled as in the vanilla
Block-STM for that particular deferred object.
4.4  Rolling commit
Block-STM uses a mechanism called lazy commit. Since the
blockchain state is updated per block, lazy commit aims
to reduce the synchronization overhead by committing all
transaction results in the block at once. This is a showstopper
for an efficient design of RapidLane, however, as revealing a
value, validating a reveal, or validating predicted update
precondition output, would all require traversing the whole
block and accumulating the encountered deltas. Another
important reason is that the longer the speculative chain is,
the less likely the predictions are going to be correct.
To overcome this challenge, we introduce a novel rolling
commit mechanism. It allows the system to determine when
a transaction will no longer be re-executed, which implies
the output is final, and can be committed.
We terminate delta traversals when a committed value is
encountered, so the complexity of traversals in RapidLane
when executing transaction tx𝑖 depends on 𝑖 − 𝑗 as opposed
to 𝑖 when the first 𝑗 transactions are committed. This aligns
perfectly with the scheduling philosophy that prioritizes
tasks with lower 𝑖 (while rolling commit increments 𝑗 ).
Beyond RapidLane, rolling commit provides fine-grained
access to the block execution. This is helpful to efficiently sup-
port scenarios when only a prefix of transactions needs to be
4
committed, e.g., when the block gas limit  is exceeded. Addi-
tionally, block execution is often followed by post-processing
tasks based on the committed transaction outputs. Rolling
commit allows these tasks to run in parallel with Block-STM,
which can improve the latency of block execution.
A transaction tx𝑖 can be committed at time 𝑇 iff:
(1)  𝑖 = 1 or transaction tx𝑖−  is committed at 𝑇 , and
1
(2)  the latest scheduled validation by time 𝑇 for transac-
tion tx𝑖 has succeeded.
As validation can be scheduled for the suffix, it is generally
impossible to know if a particular validation is the last to ever
be scheduled for transaction tx𝑖 .

The inductive condition (1)
comes into play here, as no such validations for tx𝑖 can get
scheduled after time 𝑇 , when the prefix tx , . . . , tx𝑖−  is com-
1  1
mitted. Checking condition (1) is simple and efficient, e.g., by

arallel Execution
maintaining an atomic commit_idx counter. A counter per
transaction can be used to check (2), by ordering validations
for each transaction based on their scheduling time. However,
this design requires schedule_suffix_validation(i) to
atomically traverse and update these counters for trans-
actions tx𝑖 , . . . , tx𝑛, which would be extremely inefficient.
Rolling commit avoids this inefficiency by introducing a
global validation_wave counter and instead of updating all
validation counters, having validation scheduling check the
current validation wave for ordering purposes. The details of
a correct concurrent implementation of such a mechanism
are delicate, and are explained in Appendix A.
4.5  RapidLane integration into Block-STM
In addition to vanilla Block-STM recording all reads during
a transaction execution in the CapturedReads component,
RapidLane records all deferred object reveals. In order to
commit an incarnation of tx𝑖 in which a deferred object 𝐴
was revealed to value 𝑣, RapidLane needs to ensure that the
value of 𝐴 is 𝑣 after the first 𝑖 −1 transactions in the block are
committed. Each delta in the delta-set of the incarnation also
needs to be validated. In particular, the history constraint
of the delta (in the compressed log) needs to be satisfied
by the value of the deferred object in the committed state
prior to the transaction. This constraint corresponds to an
interval, which is less strict than the equality to a specific
value for revealed deferred value, and is likely to be satisfied
as the output of delta updates are predicted based on the
latest committed value of the deferred object.
RapidLane performs these deferred object validations right
before a transaction is committed by the rolling commit
mechanism. The object values can be read efficiently, with-
out traversing any deltas, as the state before the transaction
is committed. Checking the constraints also does not involve
any heavy computation. If deferred object validation suc-
ceeds, the transaction gets committed. If validation fails, the
transaction is immediately re-executed and committed with-
out additional validation (re-execution happens from the
correct state by Property 1). The committed deferred object
values get reflected in MVDelayedFields (as deltas with re-
vealed values), allowing the delta traversals to terminate at
the committed transaction index.
As discussed previously, for every committed transaction,
Block-STM performs post-commit processing in parallel.
RapidLane utilizes this processing to incorporate the results
of all deferred updates into the write-set of the transaction
output by inserting inline the committed deferred values.
An important detail of the above integration is that it
requires a slightly stronger theoretical property than the one
claimed in the Block-STM paper [20]. Validation in vanilla
Block-STMcompares versions, which are either from storage,
or (transaction index, incarnation number) pairs associated.

RapidLane is implemented in Rust, and is integrated into the
Block-STM execution engine used by the Aptos blockchain,
with the source code being publicly available. RapidLane has
been released in production as well.
5.1  Benchmarking methodology
We used a Google Cloud t2d-standard-60 (AMD Milan CPU,
60 cores, 240GB memory), hyper-threading disabled.
We evaluated RapidLane using the benchmarking infras-
tructure of the Aptos blockchain. All benchmarks were per-
formed on a single node. Each benchmark consisted of mea-
suring the throughput of executing and committing 10 blocks,
with 10,000 transactions per block. The global state was
initialized with 200,000 accounts in order to mimic a real
blockchain setup. Additionally, 20,000 more accounts were
created, each with a sufficiently large balance to pay for
transactions. Each transaction sender was selected uniformly
at random from these additional accounts.
We consider two sets of workloads: realistic, listed in Ta-
ble 1, and synthetic, listed in Table 2. While realistic work-
loads cover a large variety of real blockchain use cases with
a high degree of contention, we also used a small set of
hand-crafted contracts in order to stress-test our system.
5.2  Realistic workloads
First, we consider blocks of no-op transactions. Because Ap-
tos tracks the total supply and burns fees for every transac-
tion, even this simple workload is fully sequential. Figure 8
shows the throughput when supply is tracked using integers,
deferred objects, or not tracked at all. Tracking supply with
integers yields a slowdown of up to 6.5× when compared
to not tracking it, whereas using deferred objects shows
competitive performance.
In the next experiment, we assume that supply is tracked
with a deferred counter, and consider a block of sponsored
transactions. Figure 9 shows the throughput for different

Transfers from a single

Using deferred objects avoids the sequential bottleneck, re-
sulting in speedups of up to 11.6×.
Note that using deferred objects does not yield perfect
scalability, as one might have expected; however, neither
does using non-conflicting transactions. They both peak
when 32 out of 60 available cores are used. This is a limitation
of the implementation of the Block-STM execution engine
we used, and it is outside of the scope of this work.
Lastly, we consider an NFT minting workload, with both
a limited (66, 000 NFTs) and unlimited collection. Both use
deferred objects to track the supply and represent the NFT
names. Figure 11 shows the obtained throughput. When the
collection is limited, RapidLane estimates if minting suc-
ceeds or not. The estimation is correct nearly at all times, as
demonstrated by the high throughput for the limited collec-
tion minting, with a speedup of up to 10.1× compared to the
sequential baseline.

5.3  Synthetic workloads
We first study how mispredicting preconditions affects the
throughput, using a block of cnt transactions, each randomly
incrementing or decrementing a deferred counter. We vary
the upper bound between 1 and 1000, and show the through-
put in Figure 12.
As expected, as the misprediction probability increases,
the throughput decreases. Even in the worst case (upper
bound of 1), with only 50% chance of predicting correctly we
obtain a speedup of up to 1.8×.
Next, we use reveal transactions. Transactions in the block
are generated in such a way that a fixed fraction of them
reveals a deferred object. Figure 13 shows the throughput. As
expected, we observe that revealing the deferred object leads
to lower performance. For instance, when 10% of transactions
do a reveal, the throughput is up to 4.4× lower thanwhen not.

There has been much prior work on executing smart con-
tracts in parallel using both pessimistic [5, 34, 64] and op-
timistic [2, 20, 45] approaches, but less attention has been
given to optimizing the execution of workloads with inherent
read-write conflicts [18, 46].
Some existing techniques exploit commutativity of oper-
ations [35, 43, 45] – a well-known technique when imple-
menting efficient concurrent data structures [24, 30]. Shard-
ing counters [24, 32] is another technique used to ensure
collision-free modifications of shared data. However, these
techniques are not directly applicable in blockchains where
execution has to be deterministic.
There exist many language abstractions for writing con-
current programs [12, 13, 15, 23, 29, 31, 39]. Loop reductions
updating a shared variable also have been extensively stud-
ied [10, 17], including automatic loop vectorization done by
compilers [6, 48]. There is alsowork on creating concurrency-
aware types [11, 25].
The concept of deferring computations has also been
used in other domains, including machine learning frame-
works [33, 53], concurrent data structure design [1, 37], and

We presented deferred objects, a new construct for smart
contract programming languages that enables conflicting
transactions (with read-write conflicts) to be run in parallel.
We designed and implemented an extension to the Block-
STM parallel execution engine to support deferred objects,
called RapidLane. We integrated RapidLane in the Aptos
blockchain and showed that it can improve the throughput
of real workloads by up to 12×.

Figure 16. Example of rolling commit trying to commit transaction
4. In the first figure, rolling commit will not succeed since the latest
validated wave is 2, which is less than the maximum triggered
wave for transaction 4 (which is equal to 3 and computed as the
maximum of triggered waves for transactions 1, 2, 3 and 4). At a
later point, when current validation wave is 6, transaction 4 gets
re-validated again and the latest validated wave gets updated to 6,
allowing transaction 4 to be committed.
A simple example of rolling commit (without required
wave being set) is given in Figure 16. A more detailed speci-
fication of the protocol is as follows:
• commit_idx is an atomic counter that records the in-
dex of the next transaction to be committed, which
gets incremented each time when a transaction gets
committed.
• validation_wave is an atomic counter that records
the current validation wave, which gets incremented
each time schedule_suffix_validation is called.
• For transaction 𝑖, the system tracks three atomic coun-
ters required_wave, triggered_wave and (latest) valid
which gets updated as follows:
– required_wave ← max(required_wave,
validation_wave)
when schedule_validation(i) is called, mean-
ing that transaction 𝑖 needs to be successfully val-
idated by a wave ≥ required_wave.
– triggered_wave ← max(triggered_wave,
validation_wave) when
schedule_suffix_validation(i) is called, mean-
ing that any transaction ≥ 𝑖 will need to be vali-
dated by a wave ≥ triggered_wave.
– validated_wave ← max(validated_wave,𝑤)
when the a validation succeeds, where 𝑤 is the
wave of the validation, assigned at start by reading
the validation_wave counter.
• commit_wave is an atomic counter that records the
highest required wave of a successful validation for
the next transaction to be committed, updated as
commit_wave ← max(commit_wave,
triggered_wave[𝑖]) when trying to commit a trans-
action 𝑖.

arallel Execution
The scheduler updates validation_wave and commit_wave
properly during re-validation and commit. To commit the
next transaction, the scheduler needs to ensure the validation
task is successful, and that the validation started sufficiently
late, i.e. belonged to a sufficiently large wave. This is accom-
plished by checking validated_wave against commit_wave
and required_wave.
More specifically, the transaction commit_idx is commit-
ted validated_wave ≥ max(commit_wave, required_wave)
holds for it, and commit_idx is incremented by 1.
Compared to lazy commit, the only additional overhead
may come from the cost of maintaining thewave information.
To minimize the overhead, our implementation concatenates
the wave number with the validation index to be the new
validation index, and use atomic operations to update the
wave number or validation index. Our evaluation in Sec-
tion 5.4 demonstrates that rolling commit adds no overhead
compared to lazy commit.
An alternative approach would be to dedicate one thread
to incrementally validate and commit transactions. Note that
this adds one extra validation (one that succeeds in order
to commit) per transaction. More importantly, when Block-
STM is used with few threads, losing one thread is significant.
However, when Block-STM is used with many threads, it is
possible for the committing thread to become the latency
bottleneck. In contrast, rolling commit interprets the results
of validations organically scheduled by vanilla Block-STM
with careful synchronization to determine when transactions
can be committed.
